steps.args: {'prompt': 'Extract every figure and table entry concerning 2024 stock-based compensation expense, especially any adjustment used to reconcile net income to Adjusted EBITDA or net cash from operations. State units, exact table label, period, and calculation context. {{airbnb_2024_results}}'}
steps.result:
The shareholder letter reports the following 2024 stock-based compensation amounts:

| Exact table label | Period | Amount | Units | Calculation context |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| **“Stock-based compensation expense”** in **“Reconciliation of Net Income to Adjusted EBITDA”** | Three months ended December 31, 2024 | **$406** | USD millions | Added back to GAAP net income, together with taxes, net interest and other items, to calculate Adjusted EBITDA. |
| **“Stock-based compensation expense”** in **“Reconciliation of Net Income to Adjusted EBITDA”** | Year ended December 31, 2024 | **$1,418** | USD millions | Full-year noncash compensation add-back in the reconciliation from GAAP net income to Adjusted EBITDA. |
| **“Stock-based compensation expense”** in **“Condensed Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows”**, under **“Adjustments to reconcile net income to net cash provided by operating activities”** | Year ended December 31, 2024 | **$1,418** | USD millions | Noncash expense added back to net income when calculating net cash provided by operating activities. |

### Reconciliation interpretation

- **Adjusted EBITDA:** the 2024 stock-based compensation adjustment was **+$1.418 billion** for the full year and **+$406 million** for Q4.
- **Operating cash flow:** the same **$1.418 billion** full-year expense was added back because it reduced GAAP net income but did not represent a current-period cash outflow.
- The **free-cash-flow reconciliation** begins with net cash provided by operating activities, so it does **not** show a separate stock-based compensation line; the adjustment is already reflected in operating cash flow.

All amounts above are presented in **millions of U.S. dollars**.

If all of this is unfamiliar to you, please refer to our post on discounted cash flows here.", "score": 0.31510386, "raw_content": null}, {"url": "https://medium.com/@MindtheNonGaap/stock-based-compensation-an-expense-or-dilution-both-a5c768f88a6b", "title": "Stock Based Compensation — an expense or dilution? Both!", "content": "The other argument that we have heard why SBC is not an expense is that companies do it because of “war for talent” or “industry practice”. steps.args: {'prompt': "For fiscal year ended Dec 31 2024, identify Airbnb's adjustment for stock-based compensation expense. Extract relevant table title, exact amount and units, whether it's added back, and comparator if shown. Focus both statement of cash flows adjustments and adjusted EBITDA reconciliation if present. Quote enough contextual wording to substantiate. {{airbnb_2024_10k}}"}
steps.result:
For Airbnb, Inc.’s fiscal year ended **December 31, 2024**, the stock-based compensation adjustment was **$1,407 million ($1.407 billion)** in both the cash-flow reconciliation and the Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation.

| Presentation | Relevant table title and context | FY2024 amount | Treatment | Comparator(s) shown |
|---|---|---:|---|---:|
| Statement of cash flows | **“Airbnb, Inc. Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows (in millions)”** under **“Adjustments to reconcile net income to cash provided by operating activities”** | **$1,407 million** | **Added back** to net income as a non-cash operating cash-flow adjustment | 2023: **$1,120 million**; 2022: **$930 million** |
| Adjusted EBITDA | **“Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation”**, described as **“a reconciliation of net income to Adjusted EBITDA (in millions, except percentages)”** | **$1,407 million** | **Added back/excluded** in calculating Adjusted EBITDA | 2023: **$1,120 million** |

### Supporting excerpts

**Cash-flow statement**

> “Airbnb, Inc. Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows (in millions)”

Under:

> “Adjustments to reconcile net income to cash provided by operating activities:”

the filing reports:

> “Stock-based compensation expense — **930** [2022], **1,120** [2023], **1,407** [2024].”

Because the positive **1,407** appears among the adjustments reconciling **net income of $2,648 million** to **net cash provided by operating activities of $4,518 million**, it is an **add-back**.

**Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation**

The filing states:

> “The following is a reconciliation of net income to Adjusted EBITDA (in millions, except percentages):”

The table reports:

> “Stock-based compensation expense — **$1,120** [2023], **$1,407** [2024].”

It reconciles **2024 net income of $2,648 million** to **Adjusted EBITDA of $4,041 million**. Airbnb further explains:

> “The above items are excluded from our Adjusted EBITDA measure because they are non-cash in nature, or because the amount and timing of these items are unpredictable, not driven by core results of operations, and renders comparisons with prior periods and competitors less meaningful.”

Thus, the FY2024 stock-based compensation expense is a **positive $1.407 billion add-back** in both presentations.

steps.args: {'prompt': "Resolve discrepancy precisely. Locate all 2024 amounts of 1,407 and 1,418 associated with stock-based compensation. Explain exact labels and why different, if both exist. Do not infer. Quote exact lines/table labels and identify authoritative answer to question 'what was Airbnb's adjustment for Stock-based Compensation Expense?' {{airbnb_2024_results}} {{airbnb_2024_10k}}"}
steps.result:
## Resolution

**Both amounts exist, but they appear in different presentations. All amounts are in millions.**

### 1. **$1,418 million — FY2024 shareholder letter**

In Airbnb’s **Q4 and Full-Year 2024 shareholder letter**, under **“Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation,”** the 2024 row is:

> “Stock-based compensation expense — $1,418”

That presentation includes **$11 million of amortization of stock-based compensation previously capitalized as internally developed software** in the stock-based-compensation adjustment. Correspondingly, the shareholder-letter reconciliation reports **$54 million** for depreciation and amortization.

Thus, in that reconciliation:

- Stock-based compensation expense: **$1,418 million**
- Depreciation and amortization: **$54 million**
- Combined: **$1,472 million**

### 2. **$1,407 million — 2024 Form 10-K**

The Form 10-K contains four exact 2024 occurrences associated with stock-based compensation.

#### A. MD&A — **“Adjusted EBITDA Reconciliation”**

> “Depreciation and amortization … 65”  
> “Stock-based compensation expense … 1,407”

Here, the $11 million amortization component remains in **“Depreciation and amortization,”** rather than being included in the stock-based-compensation row:

- Stock-based compensation expense: **$1,407 million**
- Depreciation and amortization: **$65 million**
- Combined: **$1,472 million**

Therefore, the two Adjusted EBITDA reconciliations reach the same result:

> “Adjusted EBITDA — $4,041”

#### B. Results of operations — functional expense table

The table introduced by:

> “Includes stock-based compensation expense as follows (in millions, except percentages):”

reports:

> “Operations and support … $90”  
> “Product development … 886”  
> “Sales and marketing … 170”  
> “General and administrative … 261”  
> **“Stock-based compensation expense … $1,407”**

The components total $1,407 million.

#### C. Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows

Under adjustments reconciling net income to operating cash flow:

> **“Stock-based compensation expense … 1,407”**

#### D. Note 16 — **“Segment and Geographic Information”**

Under **“The following table sets forth the Company’s significant segment expenses”**:

> **“Stock-based compensation expense … 1,407”**

Note 12 also describes the 2024 amount in rounded form:

> “Stock-based compensation expense was $930 million, $1.1 billion and $1.4 billion for the years ended December 31, 2022, 2023 and 2024, respectively.”

## Why the figures differ

The documented difference is an **$11 million classification difference**, not a difference in Adjusted EBITDA:

| FY2024 reconciliation | Stock-based compensation | Depreciation and amortization | Combined |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Shareholder letter | $1,418 | $54 | $1,472 |
| Form 10-K MD&A | $1,407 | $65 | $1,472 |

The shareholder-letter presentation includes the $11 million amortization of capitalized stock-based compensation in **“Stock-based compensation expense.”** The 10-K presentation leaves that $11 million within **“Depreciation and amortization.”**

## Authoritative answer to the exact question

If the question is:

> **“What was Airbnb’s adjustment for Stock-based Compensation Expense?”**

and it refers to the **Q4 and Full-Year 2024 shareholder letter’s Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation**, the answer is:

> **$1,418 million.**

If the question instead asks for the **2024 stock-based compensation expense reported in Airbnb’s Form 10-K tables and cash-flow statement**, the answer is:

> **$1,407 million.**

The Investor Relations press-release landing page itself does not state either amount; it links to the shareholder letter. The **$1,418 million** answer is therefore the directly applicable amount for the shareholder letter’s specifically labeled Adjusted EBITDA adjustment.
